problem_summary: Predict the 2028 Los Angeles Olympic Games medal counts per country, including event numbers, medal tallies, first-time medalists, and the impact of introducing great coaches, using historical data and providing insights for Olympic committees.
key_techniques: Grid search with 5-fold cross-validation for SVR hyperparameter tuning
Feature engineering: medal ratios, scoring system (3-2-1 weights)
Host country effect adjustment (15% score increase for USA)
Confidence interval estimation using residual normality assumption and z-score
Monte Carlo simulation for first-time medalist count distribution
Threshold method and Isolation Forest for anomaly-based coach effect detection
Ensemble prediction (Linear Regression + Random Forest) for coach impact
One Cycle Learning Rate Scheduler and Adam optimizer for LSTM training
Punishment term in LSTM loss to enforce medal count sums
why_it_won: The paper delivers a thorough, multi-model solution addressing all problem requirements with strong methodological justification. It transparently iterates from a flawed LSTM baseline to a robust XGBoost model, incorporates real-world complexities like host effect and prediction intervals, and provides actionable insights through sensitivity analysis and clear visualizations. The integration of statistical tests (Mann-Kendall), survival analysis (Cox), and anomaly detection (Isolation Forest) showcases depth and versatility, while the explicit strengths/weaknesses discussion demonstrates critical thinking.
section_highlights: The report is exceptionally well-organized with a clear progression from assumptions and notations through four distinct models, each with a dedicated overview, establishment, and results subsection. Effective use of flow charts, scatterplots, and annotated tables guides the reader, while the sensitivity analysis and strength/weakness sections reinforce credibility and transparency.
pitfalls_or_limitations: SVR model shows high MSE (1.744) for disciplines with unstable historical event counts
LSTM baseline suffered from overfitting and underfitting, predicting impossible medal counts
Assumption that all performance improvements beyond a threshold are solely due to coaches is simplistic
Host effect adjustment fixed at 15% for USA based on limited historical cases may not be accurate
The Great Coach Effect model assumes the impact pattern of a coach is consistent across different nations
reusable_patterns: Using SVR with Gaussian kernel and grid-search CV for nonlinear event count forecasting
Converting raw medal counts to winning ratios and using XGBoost to predict percentages, then multiplying by predicted event numbers
Applying Mann-Kendall trend test to identify countries with significant upward or downward medal trends
Treating first-medal attainment as a survival process with Cox proportional hazards model and Monte Carlo simulation
Combining threshold-based first-order difference/ratio with Isolation Forest to detect anomalies (coach effects)
Ensembling Linear Regression and Random Forest on detected GCE points to predict future performance under a new coach
Estimating prediction intervals by assuming normal distribution of residuals from XGBoost
